methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $10M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

data.narrative:
Exodys Energy is a seed-stage nuclear energy company founded by Carl Perez and Jean-Christophe Piroux that has raised $10M+. The valuation universe comprises 11 comparable companies: 3 publicly traded peers (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private-sector participants including Oklo, TerraPower, X-Energy, Kairos Power, and Nano Nuclear Energy.

The stage-progression method is the sole applicable triangulation approach here. Using total raised ($10M) as a proxy for seed-stage valuation and applying a typical 2.5x multiplier for progression to the next funding round yields an implied valuation of $25M. This method is anchored to observable funding trajectory patterns in early-stage nuclear technology companies.

Peer-median analysis was not viable due to insufficient comparable datapoints at the same stage and valuation tier within the narrow nuclear energy sector. The broader eight-company nuclear peer set lacks sufficient depth in the immediate comparables required for reliable cross-sectional valuation. The analysis therefore supports a valuation range of $21M to $29M, with a median of $25M. Key uncertainties include the lack of transaction comps at identical stage maturity, which limits confidence in the step-up multiplier's precision for this particular cohort, and the early-stage nature of the sector, where capital requirements and commercialization timelines remain highly variable.
